macro_segments.title: The Principle of Stam Kodshim and Its Resolution (Recap from Previous Day)

micro_segments.title: Ravina's Question to Rav Pappa: Review of Yesterday's Contradiction and Resolution
The Contradiction: Kodshim vs. Get — Why One Requires Specific Intent and the Other Doesn't
The Resolution: Stam Kodshim Lishmon — How Kedusha Defines the Act Rather Than Intent

macro_segments.title: Minah vs. Lo Minah: The Principle That Only Things of the Same Kind Destroy

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in the Name of Rav: Chatat Shechutah Leshem Olah vs. Leshem Chulin
The Principle Emerges: Minah Machrib Bah; Lo Minah Lo Machrib Bah
Challenging the Principle with a Contradiction: Get Written for a Non-Jewish Woman
Resolution: Stama Pasul by Get; Absence of Wrong Intent Versus Absence of Right Intent

macro_segments.title: Kli Cheres and the Concept of Tocho (Inside Space): Vessels vs. Walls

micro_segments.title: Biblical Source: Pottery Vessel and the Tumah of Dead Creatures (Shratzim)
The Halacha of Tocho: Food Inside a Kli Cheres Becomes Tamei via Air Space
The Question: Why Does Even a Non-Pottery Vessel (Glass) Save the Food? Shouldn't Lo Minah Not Matter?
The Answer: Asu Chulin Etzel Kodshim — A Vessel Is Still Minah; A Wall Is Truly Lo Minah

macro_segments.title: The Tannur Case: When a Mechitzah Does Not Protect — The Exception That Proves the Rule

micro_segments.title: Tannur Shechatzatzo B'Nesarim: Dividing an Oven with Planks or Curtains Does Not Create Separate Air Spaces
The Keiveret Case: A Quasi-Vessel Lowered into the Oven — Does It Serve as Mechitzah?
Rabbi Eliezer's Kal V'Chomer: Walls Protect from Tumat Hameis, So Why Not Here?
The Rabbis' Response: In the Context of Vessels, Walls Are Not the Natural Mechitzah
Resolution: The Gemara Clarifies Both Positions — Rabbis Rely on Lo Minah; Rabbi Eliezer on Kal V'Chomer

macro_segments.title: The Food Wrapped in Plaster: When Tocho Requires an Extra Verse to Define the Inner Boundary

micro_segments.title: Ochlim Shegibevam B'Tit: Food Encased in Plaster Inside a Kli Cheres — Is It Tamei?
The Logic: Since Touch Doesn't Transmit Tumah to Wrapped Food, Air Space Shouldn't Either?
The Answer: The Extra Word Tocho Teaches Us That Even Wrapped Food Is Still Tocho

macro_segments.title: Shinui Kodesh vs. Shinui Kodesh: Why Changing the Type of Chatat Is Different from Changing to Olah

micro_segments.title: The Teaching of Rav: Chatat Shechutah Leshem Chatat Kesherah; Leshem Olah Pesula
The Apparent Contradiction: Why Is a Smaller Change (Different Type of Chatat) Acceptable but a Larger One (to Olah) Not?

macro_segments.title: Shinui Be'alim: Changing the Owner — When Irrelevance Matters and When It Doesn't

micro_segments.title: Rav's Teaching: Chatat Shechutah Al Mi Shemechuyav Chatat Pesula; Al Mi Shemechuyav Olah Kesherah
The Parallel to Vessels: Thinking About Gloria (Who Needs Olah) vs. Dov (Who Needs Chatat)
The Distinction: Gloria Is So Irrelevant It's an Irrelevant Thought; Dov Is in the Same Category and Relevant

macro_segments.title: Shinui Kodesh vs. Shinui Kodesh: Why Changing the Type of Chatat Is Different from Changing to Olah (continued)

micro_segments.title: Resolution: The Torah Only Requires Intent for Chatat Generically, Not for a Specific Type of Chatat

macro_segments.title: Shinui Be'alim: Changing the Owner — When Irrelevance Matters and When It Doesn't (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Principle of Vekipper Alav: The Torah Demands Specific Intent for the Right Owner

macro_segments.title: Rav Chaviva's Question: Shinui Be'alim Compared to Toch Tocho — Applying the Minah Principle

micro_segments.title: The Question: If Gloria (Lo Minah) Doesn't Matter, Why Does a Glass Vessel (Lo Minah) Still Save?
Distinction Clarified: Gloria Is Truly Lo Minah (Different Kind of Obligation); Glass Is Minah (Still a Vessel)
The Answer: Extra Verses of Tocho Teach That Even Non-Pottery Vessels Still Count as Minah (Vessels)

macro_segments.title: Summary: The Consistent Principle — Proximity Determines Destructiveness, Tempered by Precision Requirements

micro_segments.title: The General Rule: Minah Machrib; Lo Minah Lo Machrib — Closer Things Are More Destructive
The Exception: Torah Doesn't Always Demand Maximum Precision — It Sets Its Own Thresholds
Preview: Tomorrow We Address Leshem and the Sources for Intent in Korbanos
